title: Frau Holle
about: The twenty-fourth tale (KHM 24): a hard-working stepdaughter falls down a well into the world of Mother Holle, is rewarded with gold for her diligence, while her idle stepsister earns a shower of pitch. Spelling follows the 1857 edition (Wittwe, daß, thun, gieng).
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 24. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.

sentences.t: Eine Wittwe hatte zwei Töchter, davon war die eine schön und fleißig, die andere häßlich und faul.
sentences.l: A widow had two daughters, of whom the one was beautiful and diligent, the other ugly and lazy.
sentences.n: “Wittwe” is the 1857 spelling of Witwe (‘widow’); this edition doubles the -t- and keeps older spellings throughout. “davon” here = ‘of whom, of these.’ “die eine ... die andere” = ‘the one ... the other.’

sentences.t: Sie hatte aber die häßliche und faule, weil sie ihre rechte Tochter war, viel lieber, und die andere mußte alle Arbeit thun und der Aschenputtel im Hause sein.
sentences.l: But she had the ugly and lazy one, because she was her own true daughter, much dearer, and the other had to do all the work and be the Cinderella in the house.
sentences.i: But she loved the ugly, lazy one far more, because she was her own daughter, and the other had to do all the work and be the household drudge.
sentences.n: “häßlich” is the 1857 spelling of hässlich. “lieber haben” = to prefer, love more (comparative of lieb haben). “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste; “thun” is the old spelling of tun (‘to do’) — both keep the silent -th- and -ß- that recur in this edition. “der Aschenputtel” = the drudge, Cinderella-figure (used here as a common noun).

sentences.t: Das arme Mädchen mußte sich täglich auf die große Straße bei einem Brunnen setzen, und mußte so viel spinnen, daß ihm das Blut aus den Fingern sprang.
sentences.l: The poor girl had to seat herself daily on the great road beside a well, and had to spin so much that the blood sprang out of her fingers.
sentences.i: The poor girl had to sit down every day out on the highway beside a well, and had to spin so much that the blood spurted from her fingers.
sentences.n: “ihm” refers to das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘to it’ — read it as ‘her’; the girl is meant. This neuter reference to Mädchen runs through the whole tale. “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Nun trug es sich zu, daß die Spule einmal ganz blutig war, da bückte es sich damit in den Brunnen und wollte sie abwaschen: sie sprang ihm aber aus der Hand und fiel hinab.
sentences.l: Now it came about that the spool was once quite bloody; then she bent down with it into the well and wanted to wash it off: but it sprang out of her hand and fell down.
sentences.i: Now it came to pass that the spool was once all bloody, so she stooped down with it into the well, meaning to wash it off; but it sprang out of her hand and fell in.
sentences.n: “trug es sich zu” is the separable verb sich zutragen (‘to come about, happen’), a solemn fairy-tale phrase. “es” is impersonal here; a moment later “es” = the girl (das Mädchen). “abwaschen” (to wash off) is separable (ab-).

sentences.t: Es weinte, lief zur Stiefmutter und erzählte ihr das Unglück.
sentences.l: She wept, ran to the stepmother and told her the misfortune.
sentences.n: “Es” again is the girl (neuter Mädchen). “zur” = zu der.

sentences.t: Sie schalt es aber so heftig und war so unbarmherzig, daß sie sprach „hast du die Spule hinunterfallen lassen, so hol sie auch wieder herauf.“
sentences.l: But she scolded her so violently and was so merciless that she spoke, “have you let the spool fall down, then fetch it up again too.”
sentences.i: But she scolded her so fiercely and was so merciless that she said, “If you have let the spool fall down, then fetch it up again yourself.”
sentences.n: “schalt” is the preterite of schelten (‘to scold’). “hast du ... lassen, so ...” — a conditional made by putting the verb first (‘if you have ..., then ...’). “hinunterfallen lassen” = to let fall down; “hol ... herauf” (heraufholen, to fetch up) is separable, here as the imperative hol.

sentences.t: Da gieng das Mädchen zu dem Brunnen zurück und wußte nicht was es anfangen sollte: und in seiner Herzensangst sprang es in den Brunnen hinein, um die Spule zu holen.
sentences.l: Then the girl went back to the well and did not know what she should set about: and in her heart’s anguish she sprang down into the well to fetch the spool.
sentences.i: Then the girl went back to the well and did not know what to do; and in her anguish she leapt down into the well to fetch the spool.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling of gehen/anfangen (gieng, fieng) recurs throughout the edition. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste. “was es anfangen sollte” — anfangen here has its idiomatic sense ‘what to do, how to begin.’ “um ... zu holen” = ‘in order to fetch.’

sentences.t: Es verlor die Besinnung, und als es erwachte und wieder zu sich selber kam, war es auf einer schönen Wiese wo die Sonne schien und viel tausend Blumen standen.
sentences.l: She lost consciousness, and when she awoke and came to herself again, she was on a beautiful meadow where the sun shone and many thousand flowers stood.
sentences.i: She lost her senses, and when she awoke and came to herself again, she was in a lovely meadow where the sun was shining and many thousand flowers were growing.
sentences.n: “zu sich selber kommen” = to come to oneself, regain one’s senses. “viel tausend” = ‘many thousand’ (uninflected, an older usage).

sentences.t: Auf dieser Wiese gieng es fort und kam zu einem Backofen, der war voller Brot; das Brot aber rief „ach, zieh mich raus, zieh mich raus, sonst verbrenn ich: ich bin schon längst ausgebacken.“
sentences.l: On this meadow she went on and came to a baking-oven that was full of bread; but the bread called, “oh, pull me out, pull me out, else I burn: I have long been baked through.”
sentences.i: She walked on across this meadow and came to an oven that was full of bread; and the bread cried out, “Oh, pull me out, pull me out, or I shall burn — I was done baking long ago.”
sentences.n: “gieng ... fort” (fortgehen, to go on/away) is separable. “raus” is colloquial for heraus (‘out’); “zieh ... raus” = pull out. “verbrenn” is a clipped first person (verbrenne, ‘I burn / I’ll burn up’). “ausgebacken” is the past participle of ausbacken (‘to bake through, finish baking’), separable aus-.

sentences.t: Da trat es herzu, und holte mit dem Brotschieber alles nach einander heraus.
sentences.l: Then she stepped up, and fetched everything out one after another with the bread-shovel.
sentences.i: So she stepped up and took it all out, one loaf after another, with the baker’s peel.
sentences.n: “trat” is the preterite of treten; “herzu” = ‘up to it, hither.’ “Brotschieber” is the baker’s peel, the long shovel for the oven. “holte ... heraus” (herausholen, to fetch out) is separable; “nach einander” = one after another.

sentences.t: Danach gieng es weiter und kam zu einem Baum, der hieng voll Äpfel, und rief ihm zu „ach schüttel mich, schüttel mich, wir Äpfel sind alle mit einander reif.“
sentences.l: After that she went on further and came to a tree that hung full of apples, and it called to her, “oh shake me, shake me, we apples are all together ripe.”
sentences.i: After that she went on and came to a tree that hung full of apples, and it called to her, “Oh, shake me, shake me, we apples are all ripe together.”
sentences.n: “hieng” is the 1857 spelling of hing (hängen, ‘to hang’). “rief ... zu” (zurufen, to call to) is separable and takes the dative (“ihm” = to her, the girl). “mit einander” = ‘together, one and all’ (modern miteinander).

sentences.t: Da schüttelte es den Baum, daß die Äpfel fielen als regneten sie, und schüttelte bis keiner mehr oben war; und als es alle in einen Haufen zusammengelegt hatte, gieng es wieder weiter.
sentences.l: Then she shook the tree, so that the apples fell as if they rained, and shook until not one more was up above; and when she had laid them all together into a heap, she went on again.
sentences.i: Then she shook the tree so that the apples fell as if it were raining them, and shook until not one was left above; and when she had piled them all into a heap, she went on her way again.
sentences.n: “als regneten sie” — subjunctive with the verb first, ‘as if they were raining’ (a compressed als ob). “keiner mehr” = not one more, none left. “zusammengelegt” is the past participle of zusammenlegen (‘to lay/pile together’), separable zusammen-.

sentences.t: Endlich kam es zu einem kleinen Haus, daraus guckte eine alte Frau, weil sie aber so große Zähne hatte, ward ihm angst, und es wollte fortlaufen.
sentences.l: At last she came to a little house, out of which an old woman peeped; but because she had such great teeth, she grew afraid, and wanted to run away.
sentences.i: At last she came to a little house, from which an old woman was peeping out; but because she had such big teeth, the girl grew frightened and wanted to run away.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became / grew’); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward constantly in this edition. “ward ihm angst” = ‘fear came upon her’ (an impersonal dative idiom, ihm = to the girl). “fortlaufen” (to run away) is separable fort-.

sentences.t: Die alte Frau aber rief ihm nach „was fürchtest du dich, liebes Kind? bleib bei mir, wenn du alle Arbeit im Hause ordentlich thun willst, so soll dirs gut gehn.
sentences.l: But the old woman called after her, “what are you afraid of, dear child? stay with me; if you will do all the work in the house properly, then it shall go well for you.
sentences.i: But the old woman called after her, “What are you afraid of, dear child? Stay with me; if you will do all the household work properly, you shall fare well.
sentences.n: “rief ihm nach” (nachrufen, to call after) is separable and takes the dative. “sich fürchten” = to be afraid (reflexive). “dirs” = dir es (‘to you it’); “so soll dirs gut gehn” = ‘it shall go well for you’ (impersonal dative idiom, jemandem geht es gut). “gehn” and “thun” are the old contracted/spelled forms of gehen and tun.

sentences.t: Du mußt nur Acht geben daß du mein Bett gut machst und es fleißig aufschüttelst, daß die Federn fliegen, dann schneit es in der Welt; ich bin die Frau Holle.“
sentences.l: You must only take heed that you make my bed well and shake it up diligently, so that the feathers fly; then it snows in the world: I am Mother Holle.”
sentences.i: You need only take care to make my bed well and plump it up thoroughly, so that the feathers fly; then it snows in the world. I am Mother Holle.”
sentences.n: “Acht geben” = to take heed, pay attention (a set phrase). “aufschüttelst” (aufschütteln, to shake up, plump) is separable auf-, here kept together in the daß-clause. The folk belief: when Frau Holle shakes out her featherbed, it snows on earth. “Frau Holle” = ‘Mother Holle,’ the tale’s title figure.

sentences.t: Weil die Alte ihm so gut zusprach, so faßte sich das Mädchen ein Herz, willigte ein und begab sich in ihren Dienst.
sentences.l: Because the old woman spoke so kindly to her, the girl took heart, consented and betook herself into her service.
sentences.i: Because the old woman spoke so kindly to her, the girl took heart, agreed, and entered her service.
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun). “zusprach” (zusprechen + dative, to speak kindly/encouragingly to) is separable zu-. “sich ein Herz fassen” = to take heart, pluck up courage. “willigte ein” (einwilligen, to consent) is separable ein-; “sich in Dienst begeben” = to enter service. “faßte” keeps the old -ß-.

sentences.t: Es besorgte auch alles nach ihrer Zufriedenheit, und schüttelte ihr das Bett immer gewaltig auf daß die Federn wie Schneeflocken umher flogen; dafür hatte es auch ein gut Leben bei ihr, kein böses Wort, und alle Tage Gesottenes und Gebratenes.
sentences.l: She also saw to everything to her satisfaction, and always shook up the bed for her mightily so that the feathers flew about like snowflakes; for that she had a good life with her too, no unkind word, and every day boiled and roasted fare.
sentences.i: She saw to everything to the old woman’s satisfaction, and always plumped up her bed so vigorously that the feathers flew about like snowflakes; and in return she had a good life there, never a harsh word, and boiled and roasted meat every day.
sentences.n: “schüttelte ... auf” (aufschütteln) is separable — the prefix auf jumps to the end of the clause. “nach ihrer Zufriedenheit” = to her satisfaction. “ein gut Leben” has an uninflected gut (older usage). “Gesottenes und Gebratenes” — neuter nouns made from the past participles of sieden (‘to boil’) and braten (‘to roast’): boiled and roasted dishes.

sentences.t: Nun war es eine Zeitlang bei der Frau Holle, da ward es traurig und wußte anfangs selbst nicht was ihm fehlte, endlich merkte es daß es Heimweh war; ob es ihm hier gleich viel tausendmal besser gieng als zu Haus, so hatte es doch ein Verlangen dahin.
sentences.l: Now she was for a while with Mother Holle; then she grew sad and at first did not even know herself what was lacking to her; at last she noticed that it was homesickness. Although it went a thousand times better for her here than at home, yet she had a longing thither.
sentences.i: Now she was with Mother Holle for a while; then she grew sad, and at first did not even know herself what was wrong; at last she realized it was homesickness. Though she was a thousand times better off here than at home, still she had a longing to go there.
sentences.n: “eine Zeitlang” = for a while. “was ihm fehlte” = what was wrong with her (fehlen + dative: what was lacking to her). “ob ... gleich” is an older concessive frame, ‘although’ (= obgleich split apart). “viel tausendmal besser” = a thousand times better; “ein Verlangen dahin” = a longing to go there.

sentences.t: Endlich sagte es zu ihr „ich habe den Jammer nach Haus kriegt, und wenn es mir auch noch so gut hier unten geht, so kann ich doch nicht länger bleiben, ich muß wieder hinauf zu den Meinigen.“
sentences.l: At last she said to her, “I have got the grief for home, and however well it goes for me down here, still I cannot stay any longer; I must go up again to my own people.”
sentences.i: At last she said to her, “I have come to pine for home, and however well off I am down here, I cannot stay any longer; I must go back up to my own folk.”
sentences.n: “den Jammer nach Haus kriegt” — a colloquial idiom, ‘to be seized with homesickness’ (kriegt = clipped past participle of kriegen, ‘got’). “wenn es ... auch noch so gut geht” = ‘however well it goes’ (a concessive: no matter how good). “die Meinigen” = my own people, my folk (a substantivized possessive).

sentences.t: Die Frau Holle sagte „es gefällt mir, daß du wieder nach Haus verlangst, und weil du mir so treu gedient hast, so will ich dich selbst wieder hinauf bringen.“
sentences.l: Mother Holle said, “it pleases me that you long to go home again, and because you have served me so faithfully, I will bring you up again myself.”
sentences.i: Mother Holle said, “It pleases me that you long to go home again, and because you have served me so faithfully, I will take you back up myself.”
sentences.n: “es gefällt mir” = it pleases me (gefallen + dative). “nach Haus verlangen” = to long for home. “treu gedient” — dienen (‘to serve’) takes the dative (“mir”): served me faithfully.

sentences.t: Sie nahm es darauf bei der Hand und führte es vor ein großes Thor.
sentences.l: She thereupon took her by the hand and led her before a great gate.
sentences.n: “darauf” = thereupon, after that. “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (‘gate’), with the old -th-. “bei der Hand nehmen” = to take by the hand.

sentences.t: Das Thor ward aufgethan, und wie das Mädchen gerade darunter stand, fiel ein gewaltiger Goldregen, und alles Gold blieb an ihm hängen, so daß es über und über davon bedeckt war.
sentences.l: The gate was opened, and as the girl stood right beneath it, a mighty rain of gold fell, and all the gold stayed hanging on her, so that she was covered with it over and over.
sentences.i: The gate was opened, and just as the girl was standing right beneath it, a mighty shower of gold fell, and all the gold clung to her, so that she was covered with it from head to foot.
sentences.n: “ward aufgethan” — passive with the archaic ward; “aufgethan” is the 1857 spelling of aufgetan (auftun, ‘to open’). “gewaltiger Goldregen” = a mighty rain of gold. “an ihm hängen bleiben” = to stay clinging to her. “über und über” = all over, from head to foot.

sentences.t: „Das sollst du haben, weil du so fleißig gewesen bist“ sprach die Frau Holle und gab ihm auch die Spule wieder, die ihm in den Brunnen gefallen war.
sentences.l: “That you shall have, because you have been so diligent,” spoke Mother Holle, and gave her back the spool too, which had fallen into the well for her.
sentences.i: “This you shall have, because you have been so diligent,” said Mother Holle, and gave her back the spool as well, the one that had fallen into the well.
sentences.n: “die ihm in den Brunnen gefallen war” — the dative “ihm” is a dative of interest, ‘that had fallen into the well on her.’ “gab ... wieder” (wiedergeben, to give back) is separable wieder-.

sentences.t: Darauf ward das Thor verschlossen, und das Mädchen befand sich oben auf der Welt, nicht weit von seiner Mutter Haus: und als es in den Hof kam, saß der Hahn auf dem Brunnen und rief
sentences.l: Thereupon the gate was closed, and the girl found herself up above in the world, not far from her mother’s house: and when she came into the courtyard, the cock sat on the well and crowed
sentences.i: Thereupon the gate was shut, and the girl found herself up above in the world again, not far from her mother’s house; and as she came into the yard, the cock was sitting on the well and crowed:
sentences.n: “sich befinden” = to find oneself, be (located). “seiner Mutter Haus” = her mother’s house (a genitive placed before the noun: ‘her mother’s’). “Hahn” = cock, rooster; here it crows (rief).

sentences.t:
„kikeriki,
unsere goldene Jungfrau ist wieder hie.“
sentences.l:
“cock-a-doodle-doo,
our golden maiden is here again.”
sentences.n: “kikeriki” is the German cock’s crow (‘cock-a-doodle-doo’). “hie” is an archaic form of hier (‘here’), kept for the rhyme. “Jungfrau” = maiden, young woman.

sentences.t: Da gieng es hinein zu seiner Mutter, und weil es so mit Gold bedeckt ankam, ward es von ihr und der Schwester gut aufgenommen.
sentences.l: Then she went in to her mother, and because she arrived so covered with gold, she was well received by her and the sister.
sentences.i: Then she went in to her mother, and because she arrived so covered in gold, she was warmly received by her and by her sister.
sentences.n: “gieng ... hinein” (hineingehen, to go in) is separable. “ankam” (ankommen, to arrive) is separable an-. “ward ... aufgenommen” — passive with the archaic ward; “aufgenommen” (aufnehmen, to receive, take in) is separable auf-.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen erzählte alles, was ihm begegnet war, und als die Mutter hörte wie es zu dem großen Reichthum gekommen war, wollte sie der andern häßlichen und faulen Tochter gerne dasselbe Glück verschaffen.
sentences.l: The girl told everything that had happened to her, and when the mother heard how she had come to the great riches, she wanted gladly to procure the same fortune for the other, ugly and lazy daughter.
sentences.i: The girl told everything that had happened to her, and when the mother heard how she had come by such great wealth, she was eager to procure the same good fortune for the other, ugly, lazy daughter.
sentences.n: “begegnen” = to happen to, befall (+ dative: “ihm” = to her). “Reichthum” is the 1857 spelling of Reichtum (‘riches’), with the old -th-. “verschaffen” = to procure, obtain for someone (+ dative “der Tochter”). “dasselbe” = the same.

sentences.t: Sie mußte sich an den Brunnen setzen und spinnen; und damit ihre Spule blutig ward, stach sie sich in die Finger und stieß sich die Hand in die Dornhecke.
sentences.l: She had to seat herself at the well and spin; and so that her spool would become bloody, she pricked herself in the fingers and thrust her hand into the thorn-hedge.
sentences.i: She had to sit down at the well and spin; and to make her spool bloody, she pricked her fingers and thrust her hand into the thorn-hedge.
sentences.n: “damit ... ward” — damit here is a conjunction of purpose, ‘so that’; ward is again the archaic preterite of werden. “stach” is the preterite of stechen (‘to prick’); “stieß” of stoßen (‘to thrust’). “Dornhecke” = thorn-hedge.

sentences.t: Dann warf sie die Spule in den Brunnen und sprang selber hinein.
sentences.l: Then she threw the spool into the well and sprang in herself.
sentences.n: “warf” is the preterite of werfen (‘to throw’). “sprang ... hinein” (hineinspringen, to jump in) is separable; “selber” = herself.

sentences.t: Sie kam, wie die andere, auf die schöne Wiese und gieng auf demselben Pfade weiter.
sentences.l: She came, like the other, onto the beautiful meadow and went on further on the same path.
sentences.i: She came, like the other, into the beautiful meadow and walked on along the same path.
sentences.n: “demselben Pfade” = the same path (Pfade has the old dative -e ending). “gieng ... weiter” (weitergehen, to go on) is separable weiter-.

sentences.t: Als sie zu dem Backofen gelangte, schrie das Brot wieder „ach, zieh mich raus, zieh mich raus, sonst verbrenn ich, ich bin schon längst ausgebacken.“
sentences.l: When she reached the baking-oven, the bread cried again, “oh, pull me out, pull me out, else I burn, I have long been baked through.”
sentences.i: When she reached the oven, the bread cried out again, “Oh, pull me out, pull me out, or I shall burn — I was done baking long ago.”
sentences.n: “gelangte” (gelangen) = reached, came to. “schrie” is the preterite of schreien (‘to cry out, shriek’). The bread’s plea repeats the earlier one nearly word for word.

sentences.t: Die Faule aber antwortete „da hätt ich Lust mich schmutzig zu machen,“ und gieng fort.
sentences.l: But the lazy one answered, “there I’d have a mind to make myself dirty,” and went off.
sentences.i: But the lazy one answered, “As if I’d care to get myself dirty!” and walked on.
sentences.n: “die Faule” = the lazy one (adjective used as a noun). “hätt” is a clipped subjunctive II of haben (hätte); “da hätt ich Lust” is ironic, ‘as if I’d feel like it.’ “gieng fort” (fortgehen) is separable.

sentences.t: Bald kam sie zu dem Apfelbaum, der rief „ach, schüttel mich, schüttel mich, wir Äpfel sind alle mit einander reif.“
sentences.l: Soon she came to the apple tree, which called, “oh, shake me, shake me, we apples are all together ripe.”
sentences.i: Soon she came to the apple tree, which called, “Oh, shake me, shake me, we apples are all ripe together.”
sentences.n: “Apfelbaum” = apple tree. The tree’s plea repeats its earlier call. “mit einander” = together (modern miteinander).

sentences.t: Sie antwortete aber „du kommst mir recht, es könnte mir einer auf den Kopf fallen,“ und gieng damit weiter.
sentences.l: But she answered, “you come to me just right — one of them could fall on my head,” and went on further with that.
sentences.i: But she answered, “A fine idea — one of them might fall on my head!” and with that walked on.
sentences.n: “du kommst mir recht” is ironic, ‘that’s just what I need’ / ‘a fine thing.’ “einer” here = one (of the apples). “gieng ... weiter” (weitergehen) is separable; “damit” = with that.

sentences.t: Als sie vor der Frau Holle Haus kam, fürchtete sie sich nicht, weil sie von ihren großen Zähnen schon gehört hatte, und verdingte sich gleich zu ihr.
sentences.l: When she came before Mother Holle’s house, she was not afraid, because she had already heard of her great teeth, and hired herself out to her at once.
sentences.i: When she came before Mother Holle’s house, she was not afraid, because she had already heard about her big teeth, and hired herself out to her at once.
sentences.n: “der Frau Holle Haus” = Mother Holle’s house (the genitive placed before the noun). “sich fürchten” = to be afraid. “von ihren Zähnen ... gehört” — hören von = to hear about. “verdingte sich” (sich verdingen, to hire oneself out, take service) is preterite.

sentences.t: Am ersten Tag that sie sich Gewalt an, war fleißig und folgte der Frau Holle, wenn sie ihr etwas sagte, denn sie dachte an das viele Gold, das sie ihr schenken würde; am zweiten Tag aber fieng sie schon an zu faullenzen, am dritten noch mehr, da wollte sie Morgens gar nicht aufstehen.
sentences.l: On the first day she did herself violence, was diligent and obeyed Mother Holle when she told her something, for she thought of all the gold that she would give her; but on the second day she already began to laze about, on the third still more, then in the morning she would not get up at all.
sentences.i: On the first day she forced herself, was diligent, and did as Mother Holle told her, for she was thinking of all the gold the old woman would give her; but on the second day she already began to laze about, on the third still more, and in the morning she would not get up at all.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = tun, ‘to do’). “sich Gewalt anthun” (here “that ... an”) = to force oneself, do oneself violence (separable an-). “folgen” (‘to obey’) takes the dative (“der Frau Holle”). “fieng ... an” is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable an-. “faullenzen” = the 1857 spelling of faulenzen (‘to laze, idle’). “aufstehen” (to get up) is separable auf-; “Morgens” = in the morning.

sentences.t: Sie machte auch der Frau Holle das Bett nicht wie sichs gebührte, und schüttelte es nicht, daß die Federn aufflogen.
sentences.l: She also did not make Mother Holle’s bed as was fitting, and did not shake it so that the feathers flew up.
sentences.i: Nor did she make Mother Holle’s bed as she ought, nor shake it so that the feathers flew up.
sentences.n: “der Frau Holle das Bett machen” — a dative of the person (‘to make the bed for Mother Holle’). “wie sichs gebührte” = as was proper/fitting (sich gebühren, an impersonal reflexive; sichs = sich es). “aufflogen” (auffliegen, to fly up) is separable auf-.

sentences.t: Das ward die Frau Holle bald müde und sagte ihr den Dienst auf.
sentences.l: Of that Mother Holle soon grew weary and gave her notice.
sentences.i: Of this Mother Holle soon grew weary and dismissed her from service.
sentences.n: “das ... müde werden” = to grow tired/weary of that (“das” is an accusative/genitive-like object of müde; ward is again the archaic werden). “sagte ... auf” (aufsagen, here ‘den Dienst aufsagen’ = to give notice, dismiss) is separable auf-, with the dative “ihr.”

sentences.t: Die Faule war das wohl zufrieden und meinte nun würde der Goldregen kommen; die Frau Holle führte sie auch zu dem Thor, als sie aber darunter stand, ward statt des Goldes ein großer Kessel voll Pech ausgeschüttet.
sentences.l: The lazy one was well content with that and supposed the rain of gold would now come; Mother Holle led her too to the gate, but when she stood beneath it, instead of the gold a great kettle full of pitch was poured out.
sentences.i: The lazy one was quite content with that, and supposed the shower of gold would now come; Mother Holle led her to the gate as well, but when she stood beneath it, instead of gold a great kettle full of pitch was poured out over her.
sentences.n: “war das ... zufrieden” = was content with that (zufrieden here governs the thing directly). “meinte nun würde ... kommen” — reported thought in subjunctive II (würde kommen). “statt des Goldes” = instead of the gold (statt + genitive). “ward ... ausgeschüttet” — passive with archaic ward; “ausgeschüttet” (ausschütten, to pour out) is separable aus-. “Pech” = pitch, tar.

sentences.t: „Das ist zur Belohnung deiner Dienste“ sagte die Frau Holle und schloß das Thor zu.
sentences.l: “That is in reward for your services,” said Mother Holle, and shut the gate.
sentences.i: “That is the reward for your services,” said Mother Holle, and shut the gate.
sentences.n: “zur Belohnung” = as/in reward (Belohnung, fem.). “deiner Dienste” = of your services (genitive pl.). “schloß ... zu” (zuschließen, to close, lock shut) is separable zu-; “schloß” keeps the old -ß-.

sentences.t: Da kam die Faule heim, aber sie war ganz mit Pech bedeckt, und der Hahn auf dem Brunnen, als er sie sah, rief
sentences.l: Then the lazy one came home, but she was all covered with pitch, and the cock on the well, when he saw her, crowed
sentences.i: Then the lazy one came home, but she was all covered in pitch, and the cock on the well, when he saw her, crowed:
sentences.n: “kam ... heim” (heimkommen, to come home) is separable heim-. “mit Pech bedeckt” = covered with pitch.

sentences.t:
„kikeriki,
unsere schmutzige Jungfrau ist wieder hie.“
sentences.l:
“cock-a-doodle-doo,
our dirty maiden is here again.”
sentences.n: The cock’s crow again, now “schmutzige” (dirty) in place of “goldene” (golden) — the tale’s neat mirror of reward and disgrace. “hie” = archaic hier.

sentences.t: Das Pech aber blieb fest an ihr hängen und wollte, so lange sie lebte, nicht abgehen.
sentences.l: But the pitch stayed fast clinging to her and would not, as long as she lived, come off.
sentences.i: But the pitch stayed stuck fast to her and would not come off as long as she lived.
sentences.n: “an ihr hängen bleiben” = to stay clinging to her. “abgehen” (to come off) is separable ab-; “wollte ... nicht abgehen” — here wollen means the pitch ‘would not’ come off.
